heading: NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CLVII
card_summary: This chunk consists entirely of Campanini's scholarly endnotes (notes 415–484) to his introductory essay on Francesco Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi, cataloguing Zorzi's Kabbalistic sources and comparing them with those of Johann Reuchlin. The notes document Zorzi's use of texts including the Sefer Yetzirah, the Zohar, the works of Abraham Abulafia, Yosef Gikatilla (Sha'are Orah/Porta lucis), Bachyah ben Asher, Mosheh de León, Rabbi Azriel of Gerona, and Mosheh Botarel's commentary on the Sefer Yetzirah, among many others listed without identified authors. A remarkable note (428) traces the provenance of a Hebrew manuscript formerly held at Zorzi's own convent of San Francesco della Vigna in Venice to Thomas Markenfield, the English Catholic rebel who led the "Rising of the North" against Queen Elizabeth in 1569 on behalf of Mary Queen of Scots. Campanini systematically cross-references each source with its equivalent in Reuchlin's De Arte Cabalistica, measuring the originality of Zorzi's bibliographic knowledge relative to his predecessor.
essay:
# Notes to the Introductory Essay (pp. 157–161): Zorzi's Kabbalistic Library and Its Scholarly Documentation

## Overview

This chunk comprises dense scholarly endnotes (nn. 415–484) from Campanini's introductory essay to the Bompiani edition. The notes form a sustained philological and bibliographic apparatus documenting Zorzi's Kabbalistic sources, comparing them with those of Johann Reuchlin, tracing manuscript provenance, and directing readers to modern scholarship on each text or author Zorzi cites. There is no expository prose from Zorzi himself in this chunk; the content is entirely Campanini's editorial and critical commentary.

## The Sefer Yetzirah as the Most-Cited Hebrew Text

Note 418 makes the important general claim that the *Sefer Yetzirah* (Book of Formation) is the single most frequently cited post-biblical Hebrew text in all of *De Harmonia Mundi*, with references scattered across ten distinct loci spanning all three books of the work (1,3,12; 1,4,3; 1,5,16; 1,5,17; 1,6,21; 1,7,4; 2,1,5; 2,3,5; 3,5,1; 3,8,7,3). Campanini directs readers to SCHMIDT-BIGGEMANN 1993 and CAMPANINI 2011a for the history of the *Sefer Yetzirah* in a Christian context.

Note 419 compares Zorzi's treatment to Reuchlin's opening of his own Kabbalistic catalogue with the same work, quoting Reuchlin's *De Arte Cabalistica*:

> "Utimur autem [...] Abrahae libro patris nostri, cui est inscriptio [*Sefer Yetzirah*] de creatione, quem non parum literati quidam assignant Magistro Akiba profecto nobilibus scholiis ornatum, ut qui alioquin existat reconditus et obscurus".

<cite>Note 419, p. 157, quoting Reuchlin's De Arte Cabalistica</cite>

The comparison frames Zorzi's use of the *Sefer Yetzirah* as continuous with but broader than Reuchlin's, while the note on Rabbi Samuel (in the main text fragment opening the chunk) emphasizes that Zorzi was aware of commentators on the *Sefer Yetzirah* who were versed in Platonic and Peripatetic philosophy simultaneously with Kabbalistic learning — a convergence central to Zorzi's own Christian-Neoplatonic project.

## The Zohar and Its Transmission

Note 421 quotes Reuchlin's description of the Zohar — attributed to Simeon bar Yochai, who "hid in a vast and dark cave for twenty-four years":

> "Et libro [Zohar] de splendore, que composuit Simon filius Iohai cum in quodam vasto et tenebricoso specu quatuor et viginti annos delituit".

<cite>Note 421, p. 157, quoting Reuchlin</cite>

Notes 422–426 cite specific loci in *De Harmonia Mundi* where the Zohar is invoked (2,4,4; 3,6,11; 3,8,8,2; 3,2,10; 1,6,15), with note 425 pointing to the specific Zohar passage (1,78a) that underlies one reference.

## Manuscript Provenance: The Convent of San Francesco della Vigna

Note 428 is one of the most historically remarkable in the chunk. It records the Hebrew colophon of a manuscript formerly in the library of Zorzi's own convent of San Francesco della Vigna, Venice, which was copied for a patron identified as Thomas Markenfield:

> "L'antico modello di questa copia si trovava nel deposito, a Venezia, dell'ordine dei frati di S. Francesco della Vigna e nel passato era appartenuto al grande sapiente e filosofo divino, frate Francesco Zorzi, membro dell'ordine del Santo. Questa copia è stata eseguita per conto del sapiente perfetto, il principe, mio signore e patrono, il pio Signor Tommaso Markenfield, di nazionalità inglese, cavaliere del Santo Sepolcro di Gerusalemme".

<cite>Note 428, p. 158</cite>

Campanini identifies this Thomas Markenfield with the leader of the "Rising of the North" (1569), the failed Catholic rebellion against Queen Elizabeth I in favour of Mary Queen of Scots, who was condemned to death in absentia and died destitute in Brussels in 1592. This identification, if correct, attests to the remarkable afterlife of Zorzi's Hebrew books: manuscripts from his convent's collection were being copied decades after his death for an English Catholic exile, demonstrating the prestige of Zorzi's library in international Christian Kabbalistic networks.

Note 429 records that a visit to the library of San Francesco della Vigna confirmed that the convent's rich book collection was almost entirely dispersed through Napoleonic (1810) and post-unification (1866) suppressions, with only a few Hebrew items surviving, none showing signs of use by Zorzi.

## Abraham Abulafia and Reuchlin's Corruptions

Note 431 addresses Zorzi's citation of Abraham Abulafia, noting that Reuchlin had corrupted the name to "Alaphia" — a distortion already present in Reuchlin's *De Verbo Mirifico* (1494), where Abulafia appears as "Abraham secundus cognomento Alaphias." This correction illuminates how Zorzi had access to more accurate versions of Kabbalistic bibliographic data than his German predecessor.

## Yosef Gikatilla and the Confusion in Reuchlin

Note 432 addresses *Sha'are Orah* (Gates of Light, *Porta iustitiae* in Latin) by Yosef Gikatilla, noting that Reuchlin had confused Gikatilla with a fictitious "Yosef son of Carnitol," treating them as two distinct persons:

> "Et liber [Sha'are Orah] id est portae iusticiae quem conscripsit Rabi Iosef filius Carnitolis".

<cite>Note 432, p. 159, quoting Reuchlin</cite>

Campanini refers to BLAU 1944, p. 26 for documentation of this confusion's origin in Reuchlin.

## Mosheh Botarel's Commentary on the Sefer Yetzirah

Notes 442–456 form an extended sub-series documenting Zorzi's heavy use of Mosheh Botarel's commentary on the *Sefer Yetzirah*, citing multiple specific pages from the Warsaw 1884 edition (reprinted Jerusalem 1990). The notes document Botarel's pseudo-attributions of ancient authorities, his citations from Sa'adyah Gaon (including a work titled *Even ha-filosofim*, "Stone of the Philosophers"), and his influence on both Pico della Mirandola and Zorzi. Note 446 cites G. Scholem's article on Botarel in the *Encyclopaedia Judaica* as the primary modern reference.

## Bachyah ben Asher

Notes 475–478 trace Zorzi's citations of Bachyah ben Asher's Torah commentary across several loci in *De Harmonia Mundi*, including an explicit quotation at 1,4,3 concerning the Hebrew name and properties of the chrysolite (*tarshish*):

> "Rabbi Bacchaie quoque tharsim chrysolitum interpretatur: quem hebraice det [*tarshish*] cariolic; et hunc esse coloris coelestis, aut maris elati, et eius virtutum favere ad digerendum, et impinguandum".

<cite>Note 476, p. 160, quoting De Harmonia Mundi 1,4,3</cite>

Campanini notes other possible implicit citations from Bachyah at 1,7,32; 2,5,9; 3,4,9; and 2,8,5, directing readers to MOTTOLESE 2004 for a general study of Bachyah.

## Comparison with Reuchlin as Methodological Frame

Throughout these notes, Campanini's consistent method is to quote Reuchlin's *De Arte Cabalistica* catalogue of Kabbalistic books alongside the corresponding Zorzi reference, allowing the reader to measure exactly where Zorzi's knowledge overlaps with, diverges from, or surpasses Reuchlin's. This comparative apparatus supports the introductory essay's central argument about Zorzi's place in the Christian Kabbalistic tradition: deeply indebted to Reuchlin's pioneering work yet possessing independent and sometimes superior access to Hebrew sources through his position in Venice, a city with an active Jewish community and the first great Hebrew printing house (the Bomberg press, referenced in note 429).
